checks.a_analysis: The sections present in the output (Title, Project Goals, Future Initiatives, Questions?) accurately reflect the textual content of the corresponding source slides (1, 2, 3, 28, 29). The AI-generated image alt text is acceptable per the audit instructions and does not count as fabricated content.

The source document contains contact information on slides 20 and 26 (phone numbers like 919-814-5400 and email lobbyist@sosnc.gov). However, these slides are part of the content omitted from the output document. There is no contact information present in the provided JSON output, and therefore no fabrication has occurred.

The output document is missing a massive portion of the source material. Specifically, the conversion output contains a placeholder: '{"_omitted": "15 sections omitted for token budget"}' between Section 3 (Slide 3) and Section 19 (Slide 28). This indicates that slides 4 through 27 (24 pages total) are entirely absent from the output. These pages contain the majority of the presentation's core content, including technology modernization details, ADA compliance metrics, and accessibility challenge comparisons.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slides 4 through 27 of the source document containing information on Technology Modernization, Federal Template, UX Design, ADA Title II Compliance, Mobile First, Instructional Manuals, Before/After comparisons, and Accessibility Challenges.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections array, between index 2 and index 3 (placeholder for omitted content)

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any structured data tables; it primarily consists of bulleted lists and screenshots. Therefore, this check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The sections that are actually present in the output (Sections 1, 2, 3, 19, and 20) are complete and faithfully represent all text found on the corresponding source slides. The major issue is the total omission of other sections (addressed in C3), rather than truncation within the provided sections.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Comparing the text in the provided sections against the source OCR and images reveals high fidelity with no meaningful changes in wording, dates, or numbers. For example, the 'Future Initiatives' on slide 28 match the output in Section 19 exactly.

checks.a_analysis: An examination of the provided JSON output and the source PDF slides shows that text content is not duplicated. Each slide's text appears once in its corresponding section in the JSON. For example, the bullet points under 'Project Goals' on slide 2 and 'Project Goals (continued)' on slide 3 are correctly partitioned into their respective sections without repetition.

The document structure is a flat hierarchy of slides, which is appropriate for a presentation. The document title is established in the metadata, and each slide title is treated as a section heading (H2). There are no instances of skipped heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

The source document contains screenshots of tables (e.g., slides 16 and 18). These have been correctly identified as images rather than programmatic tables, which is appropriate given they are embedded visual elements of the presentation. No programmatic table structures were corrupted.

The reading order follows the sequential order of the slides from 1 to 29. Content within complex slides, such as the grid layout on slide 28 ('Future Initiatives'), is serialized in a logical row-major order (top-left, top-center, top-right, bottom-left, etc.), which maintains the intended flow of information.

Bulleted lists on slides 2, 3, 22, and 27 are accurately converted into unordered lists in the JSON. The bolding of specific terms within these list items, such as 'Modernize' and 'Implement new web template', is correctly preserved, maintaining the semantic emphasis of the original presentation.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The provided HTML metadata indicates a significant mismatch between the heading count in the semantic.json (0) and the rendered HTML (21). While the JSON metadata field 'heading_count' indicates 20, the programmatic analysis of the JSON elements themselves reports 0 heading-type objects. This 100% mismatch indicates a failure in how headings are explicitly defined in the JSON structure compared to the final output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains approximately 20 slide titles which function as primary headings for their respective content blocks.
checks.b_evidence_output: Headings in semantic.json: 0; Headings in rendered HTML: 21

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains multiple clear URLs and contact links, such as 'lobbyist@sosnc.gov' and '919-814-5270' on slide 26, and 'sosnc.gov/rulemaking' on slide 25. The JSON output fails to capture these as functional link elements, with the HTML metadata reporting 0 links in the JSON and only 1 link in the final rendered HTML. This is a significant loss of navigational utility present in the source.
checks.b_evidence_output: Links in semantic.json: 0; Links in rendered HTML: 1

checks.a_analysis: The converted HTML fails to maintain the visual organization and layout of the source presentation. Significant structural elements like the 2x3 grid on page 28 ('Future Initiatives') have been flattened into a simple vertical list. Furthermore, many pages that were primarily visual comparisons (e.g., pages 12, 13, 15, 18, 20, 21, 23, 25, 26) are either missing entirely or reduced to just a title because their visual content was not converted, breaking the document's intended visual flow.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 28 shows a grid of six boxes for 'Future Initiatives'. Pages 12, 15, 18 show side-by-side image comparisons.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'Future Initiatives' section is a vertical list. The content from slides like page 12 is entirely absent.
checks.b_evidence_location: Future Initiatives section and various missing image-based slides

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain native PDF tables; the tables present (e.g., on pages 16 and 18) are part of screenshots of a website. These screenshots are missing in the output, but there are no actual converted HTML tables to evaluate for rendering quality.

The conversion fails to include a vast majority of the meaningful images and screenshots that comprise the presentation. Examples include the 'Evolution of the Mobile Phone' image (p. 8), the 'PDF vs HTML' comparison images (p. 9), and nearly all 'Before and After' website screenshots (p. 12, 17, 18, 23, etc.) which are critical to the document's purpose.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 8: Evolution of the Mobile Phone graphic. Page 9: PDF vs HTML and mobile mockup images. Page 12: Before and After homepage screenshots.
checks.b_evidence_output: The corresponding sections in the HTML only contain text headers; the images themselves are missing.

checks.a_analysis: No visual duplication of content blocks was observed in the rendered HTML.

The overall quality is degraded due to the loss of almost all visual elements and icons (like the large question mark on the final slide). Additionally, the rendering of the title on the first page is poor, with the text wrapping awkwardly compared to the clean layout of the source slide.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 1 title 'North Carolina Department of the Secretary of State' is clean and centered. Slide 29 has a large blue question mark icon.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML title text has poor spacing and alignment. The question mark icon is missing on the final page.
checks.b_evidence_location: Title section (beginning of document) and Questions? section (end of document)
